question_title: What will be the total victim count listed for the World Leaks group on ransomware.live on July 10, 2026?
summary: Discrete count question on World Leaks victim total shown on ransomware.live on July 10, 2026. I verified the current count directly via the ransomware.live API (v2/groupvictims/worldleaks): exactly 169 victims, most recent dated 2026-06-20 (10-day inactivity gap). Horizon to July 10 is ~10 days. I built an empirical distribution by computing rolling 10-day-window additions from the actual per-victim timestamps, blending the recent-150-day regime (mean 3.6/window), full history (mean 4.2, median 2-3, max ~20), and a continued-inactivity component. Bootstrap gives mean 172.4, P50=171, P90=177, ~12% mass at the 169 floor (counts are monotonic). The distribution is right-skewed with a hard floor at 169; I widened the upper tail slightly (P99≈188) beyond the raw sim to allow for a rare large batch dump given the open upper bound. Other trackers (142/127/116) count differently and are explicitly irrelevant per the criteria.

factors.description: ransomware.live API confirms current count is exactly 169, most recent victim 2026-06-20 (verified directly)
Empirical 10-day-window additions from actual victim timestamps: recent-regime mean 3.6, full-history mean 4.2, median 2-3
Currently 10 days since last victim (inactive); bursty posting means quiet spells common but bursts follow
Historical max 10-day burst was ~20 victims; rare large dump could push toward 185-190 (open upper bound)

reasoning:
Forecast submitted.

**Summary:** The World Leaks victim count on ransomware.live is currently **169** (verified directly via their API, with the most recent victim dated 2026-06-20 — a 10-day inactivity gap). Over the ~10-day window to July 10, I built an empirical distribution from the actual per-victim discovery timestamps (rolling 10-day windows, blending recent and full-history regimes plus a continued-inactivity component).

**Distribution:**
- Floor: 169 (counts are monotonic; ~12% mass stays here)
- Median: **171**
- P90: 178, P99: ~188
- Right-skewed with an open upper tail for a rare large batch dump

The key methodological correction (prompted by the premortem reviewer) was verifying the anchor against the named resolution source rather than relying on the question description — the ransomware.live API confirmed 169 and provided the timestamps needed to ground the cadence empirically.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to the integer shown in the "Victims" statistic on the World Leaks group profile at https://www.ransomware.live/group/worldleaks as observed on July 10, 2026 (UTC). For example, if the page shows "Victims: 175", the question resolves 175.
fine_print: If the "Victims" number is not displayed on that page on July 10, 2026 but the individual victim entries are listed, the count is the number of distinct victim entries shown. If ransomware.live is entirely unavailable on July 10, 2026, the resolver will use the value shown on the first day it is reachable thereafter, up to July 17, 2026; if it remains unreachable through that date, the question is annulled. If the group page is removed or renamed, the question is annulled. Discrepancies with other trackers are irrelevant; only the count shown on ransomware.live governs.

factors.description: Current count 169 as of June 27; counts are monotonic increasing (entries rarely removed), so 169 is an effective floor
Recent posting rate ~10/month = ~0.33/day; over 13 days (June 27->July 10) implies ~4 additions
Group flagged 'Inactive Since: 7 days' in late June; near-term posting may be slow, raising probability of little/no growth
Posting is bursty (batch dumps); a single dump could add 10+ in the window

input.anchor: 169 (current count) + ~4 expected additions over 13 days at ~10/month rate = ~173
input.assessment: Discrete count question. Current value 169 (June 27). 13 days to resolution. Recent rate ~10/month implies ~4 additions, but 7-day inactivity flag and bursty posting create a right-skewed distribution with a hard floor near 169. Two Monte Carlo variants (different dormancy weights) both give mean ~173, P50=172, P90~180, P99~188. Open upper bound; tiny mass above 196.5 (~0.1-0.2%).
input.key_uncertainties: Whether the 7-day inactivity continues (keeps count near 169) or resumes with a burst (pushes toward 180+). Also whether any large batch dump occurs in the window.
input.tool_audit: execute_code: ran two Monte Carlo simulations, both converged consistently. No external data tools needed — question provides current count and trajectory directly. No failures.
input.process_reflection: Straightforward count-extrapolation question. The provided description had all needed data (current count, rate, inactivity flag), so research delegation was unnecessary. Monte Carlo with burst modeling fit well. Scaffolding was adequate; the main judgment call was dormancy weighting, handled via scenario mixture.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
WHAT I CHECKED

Trace (trace_at_premortem.md): The session contains exactly three actions — two execute_code calls (Monte Carlo simulations) and one reflection. There is NO web_search, NO fetch_url, NO content_extract. The ground-truth Tool Metrics confirm this: 3 calls total (2 execute_code, 1 reflection), 0 errors. The agent never retrieved any external data.

THE CORE PROBLEM — unverified anchor for a directly-checkable source question

This question's resolution is mechanically determined by a single integer shown on a specific live page (https://www.ransomware.live/group/worldleaks) on July 10. The resolution criteria explicitly name that page as the sole authoritative source. Unlike a behavioral/definitional question where institutional priors can substitute for a source check, here the entire numeric output is a function of (a) the current count and (b) the addition rate — both trivially checkable on that very page.

The three factual premises that drive the whole forecast appear NOWHERE in the trace:
- Factor 1: "Current count 169 as of June 27" — hard-coded as start_count=169 in both simulations with no retrieval.
- Factor 3: "Group flagged 'Inactive Since: 7 days' in late June" — no trace support.
- Factor 2: "Recent posting rate ~10/month" — no trace support.

These came from the question description or the agent's memory, not from any tool result. The agent's tool_audit claim — "No external data tools needed — question provides current count and trajectory directly" — is misleading: the resolution criteria provide only the URL, not the count or the rate. The agent had a directly fetchable authoritative source and consulted nothing.

WHY THIS IS FORECAST-AFFECTING (not just process)

1. The entire discrete distribution is anchored on 169. Every percentile (P50=172, P90=180) shifts integer-for-integer with the starting value. If the true current count differs, the whole forecast is displaced.

2. Three days of unmodeled drift already exist at publish time. "Current" data is June 27; the question published June 30. At the agent's own ~0.33/day rate, the count on publish day could already be ~170, and the "169 floor" (Factor 1, confidence 0.95) may already be stale. A single fetch would have resolved this.

3. Monotonicity is asserted but unverified. Factor 1 ("entries rarely removed") is plausible but ransomware trackers do delist victims; the fine print itself contemplates removal/renaming (annulment). No grounding.

Because this is a discrete question scored on integer accuracy, an unverified starting integer plus an unverified rate can easily move the center by several integers (e.g., if true current is already 172–175, or if a batch dump already landed). The methodology (right-skewed Monte Carlo with a near-term floor) is reasonable, and the rough center (~172) is defensible IF 169 is approximately right — but the agent never established that it is, when one fetch would have.

LESSER ISSUES
- tool_audit overstates self-sufficiency ("question provides current count directly").
- confidence_in_estimate 0.80 is somewhat high given the anchor itself was never verified.
- Counterargument is genuinely strong (coherent bimodal alternative: batch-dump upside vs. dormant/rebrand at 169) — not a token gesture. This is the forecast's strength.

RECOMMENDATION
The agent should fetch https://www.ransomware.live/group/worldleaks (or a cached/search snapshot) to confirm the current count, the inactivity status, and the recent posting cadence before committing. If 169 is confirmed and growth has been minimal, the ~172 center stands. But submitting an integer forecast for a live-page resolution without ever observing the page — when the page is the named authoritative source and was directly accessible — is a fail: the central anchor is ungrounded and the resulting integer could be off in a way that directly affects scoring.

factors.description: ransomware.live API confirms current count is exactly 169, most recent victim 2026-06-20 (verified directly)
Empirical 10-day-window additions from actual victim timestamps: recent-regime mean 3.6, full-history mean 4.2, median 2-3
Currently 10 days since last victim (inactive); bursty posting means quiet spells common but bursts follow
Historical max 10-day burst was ~20 victims; rare large dump could push toward 185-190

input.assessment: Discrete count. Verified current count = 169 via ransomware.live API; last victim June 20 (10-day gap). Horizon to July 10 = 10 days. Empirical bootstrap from actual daily victim timestamps (rolling 10-day windows, blending recent-150d and full-history regimes plus a continued-inactivity component) gives mean 172.4, P50=171, P90=177, ~12% mass at the 169 floor, P(>196.5)≈0. Distribution is right-skewed with a hard floor at 169 (monotonic count). I widen the upper tail slightly beyond the raw sim (P99 184->188) to allow for rare large dumps and model uncertainty given the open upper bound.
input.key_uncertainties: Whether the current 10-day inactivity continues (keeps count at 169) or a burst lands (could add 5-15). The open upper bound means a rare mega-dump is the main tail risk.
input.tool_audit: ransomware.live API (v2/groupvictims/worldleaks) returned full 169-victim list with timestamps — authoritative source directly verified. fetch_url on the HTML page failed to extract count (JS-rendered). web_search showed other trackers (142,127,116) which the question says to ignore. execute_code built empirical window distribution. One 429 rate-limit on a repeat API call, worked around by reusing persisted data.
input.process_reflection: The premortem reviewer correctly caught that I had not verified the anchor for a live-page-resolution question. Fetching the ransomware.live API resolved this and let me build the distribution from real per-victim timestamps rather than the description's summary rate. This materially tightened and grounded the forecast. The API was the key — HTML fetch was useless due to JS rendering.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
All four factors are directly grounded in the trace. Verified against actual tool results:

- Factor 1 (count=169, last victim 2026-06-20): Confirmed by API call `v2/groupvictims/worldleaks -> list len 169` with most recent timestamp 2026-06-20. The authoritative source named in the resolution criteria (ransomware.live) was queried directly.
- Factor 2 (recent mean 3.6, full mean 4.2, median 2-3): Confirmed — `10-day window stats: mean 4.22 median 3.0`; `Recent-150d 10-day window: mean 3.59`.
- Factor 3 (10 days inactive): Confirmed — `days since last victim: 10`.
- Factor 4 (historical max 10-day burst ~20): Confirmed — full-history `P99 20.0`.

No hallucinated evidence, no double-counting (factors draw on distinct slices: current count, empirical window distribution, current-gap state, burst tail), no wrong-direction factors (all logit=0 for a discrete distribution, appropriate). 

Resolution alignment is strong: the agent used the exact source the criteria specify, explicitly ignored the discrepant trackers (142/127/116) as the criteria instruct, and respected the monotonic hard floor at 169. Not a pre-publication-event case (forward-looking cumulative count). 

Tool audit is honest: the 429 rate-limit is real (line 182) and was worked around by reusing persisted data; the only error in the metrics is the premortem call itself (this review).

Probability assessment: The final estimate (center 171, range 169-178) faithfully reflects the empirical bootstrap (mean 172.4, P50 171, P90 177, ~12.5% mass at floor). My independent estimate lands in the same place. The minor early-sim horizon of 13 days was superseded by the 10-day-window bootstrap, which matches the true ~10-day horizon — no impact.

Counterargument is genuinely two-sided and evidence-based (upside burst to 185-189 from 90-day rate of 30 and max-burst 20; downside 169-holds from Jan-2026 low of 3 and rebrand history). Self-confidence 0.85 is calibrated to the verified anchor plus open-upper-tail uncertainty. No errors found.
